title: ChatGPT vs Claude
subtitle: Which AI assistant is right for you?
verdict: ChatGPT is the better all-rounder if you want one tool for writing, code, images, and voice. Claude is sharper for long documents, careful prose, and code review. Most pros pay for both.

dimensions.name: Writing Quality
dimensions.explanation: Claude produces more nuanced, natural prose with fewer stock phrases. ChatGPT is fast and versatile but feels more generic on long-form drafts.

dimensions.name: Coding Help
dimensions.explanation: ChatGPT with Code Interpreter slightly edges out for running and debugging code in-place. Claude wins on multi-file refactors thanks to long context.

dimensions.name: Long Documents
dimensions.explanation: Claude reads 200K+ tokens reliably. ChatGPT's effective context is shorter and degrades faster on full books or codebases.

dimensions.name: Multimodal
dimensions.explanation: ChatGPT handles voice, images, video frames, and even file uploads natively. Claude is text-and-image only.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Both cost $20/month at the Plus/Pro tiers. ChatGPT Pro at $200/month is the only big spread.

dimensions.name: Integrations
dimensions.explanation: ChatGPT plugs into thousands of third-party tools and has a Custom GPT marketplace. Claude's MCP ecosystem is younger.

quickPicks.label: Choose ChatGPT if…
points: You want one tool that does writing, code, images, and voice
You use third-party integrations or build Custom GPTs
You need fast multimodal understanding (PDFs, screenshots, audio)

quickPicks.label: Choose Claude if…
points: You work with long documents, books, or full codebases
You want more thoughtful, natural-sounding writing
Code review and refactoring is your main use case

title: ChatGPT vs Gemini
subtitle: OpenAI's all-rounder vs Google's Workspace-native AI
verdict: ChatGPT wins on output quality and ecosystem. Gemini wins if you live in Google Docs, Gmail, and Sheets — the integration there is unmatched.

dimensions.name: Output Quality
dimensions.explanation: ChatGPT consistently produces sharper, more polished output across writing, coding, and analysis. Gemini still hallucinates more in casual use.

dimensions.name: Workspace Integration
dimensions.explanation: Gemini lives inside Gmail, Docs, Sheets, and Slides. ChatGPT requires copy-paste or extensions to do the same.

dimensions.name: Mobile Experience
dimensions.explanation: Both have polished mobile apps. Gemini has tighter Android integration; ChatGPT's iOS app is slightly more refined.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Both are $20/month at the consumer tier. Google One AI Premium bundles Gemini with 2TB storage which can shift the math.

dimensions.name: Live Web Search
dimensions.explanation: Gemini's grounding in Google Search returns fresher, more accurate citations. ChatGPT's web mode improved but trails.

dimensions.name: Custom Models / GPTs
dimensions.explanation: Custom GPTs and the Apps SDK make ChatGPT extendable in ways Gemini can't yet match.

quickPicks.label: Choose ChatGPT if…
points: You want the highest output quality across all tasks
You build or use Custom GPTs and integrations
You don't rely on Google Workspace day-to-day

quickPicks.label: Choose Gemini if…
points: You live in Gmail, Docs, and Sheets
You're already a Google One subscriber
You need fresh, citation-backed web answers

title: Claude vs Gemini
subtitle: Anthropic's careful writer vs Google's Workspace AI
verdict: Claude wins on careful writing and long-document reasoning. Gemini wins if Workspace integration matters more than raw quality.

dimensions.name: Writing Quality
dimensions.explanation: Claude's prose is more natural and less repetitive. Gemini sometimes feels formulaic on long-form writing.

dimensions.name: Long Documents
dimensions.explanation: Both push 1M-token context on top tiers. Claude's recall in long contexts is slightly better in head-to-head needle tests.

dimensions.name: Workspace Integration
dimensions.explanation: Gemini is native in Google Workspace. Claude is browser-only with no first-party doc/email integration.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Both $20/month at consumer tiers; both offer enterprise plans with similar prices.

dimensions.name: Code Review
dimensions.explanation: Claude's long-context reading and Artifacts make code review more productive than Gemini for full-file edits.

dimensions.name: Mobile Experience
dimensions.explanation: Gemini ships first-class Android integration. Claude's mobile app is functional but less ambitious.

quickPicks.label: Choose Claude if…
points: You write or review long documents
You care about prose quality and tone
You do most of your work in a browser, not Google Workspace

quickPicks.label: Choose Gemini if…
points: Google Workspace is your daily driver
You use Android and want OS-level AI
You want web grounding more than thoughtful writing

title: Midjourney vs DALL·E 3
subtitle: Best-in-class artistic style vs prompt fidelity (and text in images)
verdict: Midjourney wins on artistic quality. DALL·E 3 wins on prompt fidelity, text inside images, and bundling with ChatGPT.

dimensions.name: Artistic Quality
dimensions.explanation: Midjourney V6/V7 is on a different planet for stylized and aesthetic output. DALL·E results are competent but less striking.

dimensions.name: Prompt Fidelity
dimensions.explanation: DALL·E 3 follows complex multi-clause prompts more literally. Midjourney sometimes ignores constraints in favor of style.

dimensions.name: Text in Images
dimensions.explanation: DALL·E renders readable text reliably. Midjourney still struggles with anything beyond a few clean letters.

dimensions.name: Iteration
dimensions.explanation: Inside ChatGPT you can say 'make it bluer' and DALL·E adjusts. Midjourney requires re-prompting from scratch.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: DALL·E is bundled with ChatGPT Plus at $20/month total. Midjourney is $10 minimum and $30+ for serious commercial use.

dimensions.name: Style Consistency
dimensions.explanation: Midjourney's style references (--sref) lock a look across an entire project. DALL·E has no equivalent.

quickPicks.label: Choose Midjourney if…
points: You care deeply about artistic and stylistic quality
You want to lock a consistent visual look across a project
You're producing concept art, mood boards, or marketing visuals

quickPicks.label: Choose DALL·E 3 if…
points: You need readable text inside images
You already pay for ChatGPT and don't want a second subscription
Prompt fidelity matters more than artistic flair

title: Midjourney vs Stable Diffusion
subtitle: Polished hosted product vs unlimited open-source control
verdict: Midjourney wins out-of-box quality with zero setup. Stable Diffusion wins when you want unlimited generation, full control, or commercial-grade fine-tuning.

dimensions.name: Out-of-box Quality
dimensions.explanation: Midjourney just works — type a prompt, get a stunning image. SD requires prompt engineering and model selection to match it.

dimensions.name: Cost at Scale
dimensions.explanation: Stable Diffusion is free to run locally. Midjourney's per-month cost adds up fast for high-volume users.

dimensions.name: Control & Customization
dimensions.explanation: SD has ControlNet, LoRAs, custom samplers, fine-tuning, and a vast ecosystem. Midjourney is intentionally locked down.

dimensions.name: Setup Effort
dimensions.explanation: Midjourney works in 30 seconds. SD needs a GPU, an interface (ComfyUI/A1111), models, and ongoing maintenance.

dimensions.name: Privacy
dimensions.explanation: Run SD fully offline — your prompts never leave your machine. Midjourney logs everything.

dimensions.name: Community Examples
dimensions.explanation: Both have huge communities. Midjourney's public Discord makes prompt learning easier; SD's Civitai has more model variety.

quickPicks.label: Choose Midjourney if…
points: You want stunning results in 30 seconds with no setup
You don't have a strong GPU or want to fiddle with infrastructure
You produce moderate volumes (a few hundred images/month)

quickPicks.label: Choose Stable Diffusion if…
points: You generate at high volume or need unlimited generation
You want privacy and on-device control
You need fine-tunes, ControlNet, or LoRAs for a specific look

title: Cursor vs GitHub Copilot
subtitle: AI-first editor vs the autocomplete that started it all
verdict: Cursor wins on agentic multi-file edits and chat UX. Copilot wins on integration breadth, JetBrains support, and cost — especially if you're a student.

dimensions.name: Agentic Edits
dimensions.explanation: Cursor's Cmd+K and Composer reliably make multi-file changes in one prompt. Copilot's chat-driven edits are catching up but trail.

dimensions.name: Inline Autocomplete
dimensions.explanation: Both predict your next line confidently. Cursor's Tab edits multiple lines; Copilot's NES (next edit suggestions) closed the gap.

dimensions.name: IDE Support
dimensions.explanation: Copilot supports JetBrains, Visual Studio, Vim, and more. Cursor is a VS Code fork — only one editor option.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Copilot is $10/month and free for students/OSS maintainers. Cursor's Pro tier is $20/month with stricter request caps.

dimensions.name: GitHub Integration
dimensions.explanation: Copilot's PR review, issue summarization, and CLI are deeply tied to GitHub. Cursor handles Git but isn't GitHub-native.

dimensions.name: Speed
dimensions.explanation: Cursor's Apply Diff and tab-to-accept feel snappier in day-to-day editing. Copilot's chat round-trips can lag.

quickPicks.label: Choose Cursor if…
points: You want AI baked into the editor with agent-style multi-file edits
You're happy in VS Code and want the cleanest AI UX available
You do a lot of refactoring or new feature work

quickPicks.label: Choose GitHub Copilot if…
points: You use JetBrains, Visual Studio, or Vim
Cost matters — especially if you qualify for student/OSS free
You live in GitHub PRs and want AI tied to your repo

title: Runway vs Pika
subtitle: Pro video toolkit vs short-form effects machine
verdict: Runway is the clear pro choice — better quality, more control, and built-in editing. Pika is a fun, cheaper option for short, stylized social clips.

dimensions.name: Video Quality
dimensions.explanation: Runway Gen-3/Gen-4 produces more cinematic, realistic motion. Pika is solid but plateaus on complex scenes.

dimensions.name: Motion Control
dimensions.explanation: Runway's motion brush and camera controls give precise direction. Pika offers fewer levers.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Pika starts at $10/month with usable credits. Runway's $15 entry runs out fast for serious work.

dimensions.name: Effects & Fun
dimensions.explanation: Pika's Pikaffects (crush, inflate, melt) are uniquely fun for social. Runway is more serious.

dimensions.name: Editing Tools
dimensions.explanation: Runway includes green-screen, inpainting, and a real timeline editor. Pika is generation-only.

dimensions.name: Lip Sync
dimensions.explanation: Pika's lip sync is reliable for character animation. Runway's is improving but less consistent.

quickPicks.label: Choose Runway if…
points: You're producing professional or cinematic video
You need editing tools alongside generation
Quality matters more than per-clip cost

quickPicks.label: Choose Pika if…
points: You make fun, stylized, short-form content
Lip sync for character animation is core
You want a low monthly cost with playful effects

title: ElevenLabs vs Descript
subtitle: Best-in-class voice generation vs all-in-one podcast/video editor
verdict: These solve different problems. ElevenLabs makes synthetic voices; Descript is the editor that uses them. Most podcasters end up with both.

dimensions.name: Voice Generation Quality
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs voices are the most natural in the industry. Descript's Overdub is fine but trails noticeably.

dimensions.name: Editing Tools
dimensions.explanation: Descript has full transcript-based editing, multitrack, and a video editor. ElevenLabs has no editor at all.

dimensions.name: Voice Cloning
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs clones from 1 minute of sample. Descript's Overdub clones too but quality is lower.

dimensions.name: Multilingual
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs supports 30+ languages with native inflection. Descript focuses on English.

dimensions.name: Podcasting Workflow
dimensions.explanation: Descript is built for podcasters end-to-end — record, transcribe, edit, publish. ElevenLabs is one piece of that.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs starts at $5/month. Descript starts at $12/month. Both have free tiers.

quickPicks.label: Choose ElevenLabs if…
points: You need professional voiceover for video, animation, or dubbing
Multi-language voices are required
You want voice cloning at the highest quality

quickPicks.label: Choose Descript if…
points: You're producing podcasts or YouTube videos
Editing on the transcript is your main workflow
You need a single tool for record, transcribe, edit, and publish

title: Notion AI vs Grammarly
subtitle: AI inside your wiki vs writing assistance everywhere you type
verdict: Different tools for different jobs. Notion AI is for generating and querying content inside your workspace. Grammarly is for catching tone and clarity issues across every app you write in.

dimensions.name: Writing Generation
dimensions.explanation: Notion AI writes and rewrites whole sections inside your docs. Grammarly's gen AI is more limited and bolt-on.

dimensions.name: Grammar & Tone
dimensions.explanation: Grammarly catches subtle tone and clarity issues no LLM does as reliably. Notion AI focuses on generation, not polish.

dimensions.name: Where It Works
dimensions.explanation: Grammarly works in browsers, MS Office, mobile keyboard — everywhere. Notion AI only works in Notion.

dimensions.name: Workspace Search
dimensions.explanation: Notion AI searches and answers across your whole workspace. Grammarly has no such feature.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Notion AI is a $10/month add-on. Grammarly Premium is $30/month — much pricier for what it does.

dimensions.name: Privacy
dimensions.explanation: Both send content to servers. Notion has clearer enterprise controls; Grammarly has had past privacy concerns.

quickPicks.label: Choose Notion AI if…
points: You already use Notion for notes, wiki, or docs
You want Q&A across your workspace
You generate and rewrite long content

quickPicks.label: Choose Grammarly if…
points: You write in many apps (Gmail, Slack, Word, Docs)
Tone and clarity matter for your work
You need real-time corrections as you type

title: Jasper vs Copy.ai
subtitle: Two marketing-focused content tools — which one fits?
verdict: Jasper is the enterprise pick with stronger team and brand-voice features. Copy.ai is friendlier and cheaper to start, with workflow templates that string steps together.

dimensions.name: Brand Voice
dimensions.explanation: Jasper's Brand Voice trains on your existing copy and stays on-brand at scale. Copy.ai has a simpler equivalent.

dimensions.name: Workflow Templates
dimensions.explanation: Copy.ai's workflow builder strings multiple AI steps together (research → outline → draft). Jasper is more freeform.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Copy.ai has a real free tier and starts at $49/month. Jasper has no free tier and also starts at $49.

dimensions.name: Team Features
dimensions.explanation: Jasper's team workflows, approval flows, and SSO are more enterprise-ready. Copy.ai is leaner for teams.

dimensions.name: Output Quality
dimensions.explanation: Both produce solid marketing copy that needs editing. Neither matches a senior copywriter, both beat a junior.

dimensions.name: Vs General LLMs
dimensions.explanation: Both face pressure from ChatGPT/Claude with custom prompts. Templates and brand voice are the differentiators.

quickPicks.label: Choose Jasper if…
points: You're an agency or marketing team with multiple writers
Brand consistency at scale is critical
You need approval workflows and SSO

quickPicks.label: Choose Copy.ai if…
points: You're a solo marketer or small team
You want templated workflows that handle multi-step tasks
You want to test for free before committing

title: Suno vs ElevenLabs
subtitle: Music generation vs voice generation — different tools, often paired
verdict: Suno generates full songs with lyrics and vocals. ElevenLabs generates spoken voiceover. They're complementary, not competitive — use both for video and animation.

dimensions.name: Output Type
dimensions.explanation: Suno makes songs; ElevenLabs makes spoken voice. Neither does the other's job at all.

dimensions.name: Music Quality
dimensions.explanation: Suno produces full instrumental + vocal songs. ElevenLabs has limited music tools and isn't built for it.

dimensions.name: Voice Quality
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs is best-in-class for natural speech. Suno's vocals are sung, not spoken.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Both start free. Pro tiers similar at $10–$22/month range.

dimensions.name: Commercial Use
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs Creator plan covers commercial output cleanly. Suno commercial rights only on Pro and above with active legal questions.

dimensions.name: Use Together
dimensions.explanation: Most video creators use Suno for theme music and ElevenLabs for narration in the same project.

quickPicks.label: Choose Suno if…
points: You need theme music, jingles, or full songs
You're scoring a video, podcast, or game
You don't need spoken narration

quickPicks.label: Choose ElevenLabs if…
points: You need spoken voiceover or narration
You're dubbing video into other languages
Voice cloning is part of your workflow

title: Perplexity vs ChatGPT
subtitle: Citation-first answer engine vs general AI assistant
verdict: Perplexity wins decisively for research tasks where you need cited, current sources. ChatGPT is the better general assistant for everything else.

dimensions.name: Citations
dimensions.explanation: Perplexity numbers every source by default. ChatGPT's web mode has citations but they're less reliable and harder to verify.

dimensions.name: Live Web Search
dimensions.explanation: Perplexity is built on real-time search. ChatGPT has it but it's slower and sometimes returns stale results.

dimensions.name: General Tasks
dimensions.explanation: ChatGPT is much better for writing, code, image generation, and conversation. Perplexity is research-first.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Both are $20/month at the Pro tier with similar free tiers.

dimensions.name: Multimodal
dimensions.explanation: ChatGPT handles images, voice, files, and PDFs natively. Perplexity is text-and-image focused.

dimensions.name: Following Up
dimensions.explanation: Perplexity's threaded follow-ups stay grounded in sources. ChatGPT can drift away from cited evidence.

quickPicks.label: Choose Perplexity if…
points: Research is your main use case
Citations and source verification matter
You need fresh, current information

quickPicks.label: Choose ChatGPT if…
points: You need a general-purpose assistant
You want image, voice, or code support
You build with Custom GPTs or third-party tools

title: Canva AI vs Adobe Firefly
subtitle: All-in-one design suite vs commercial-safe AI in Creative Cloud
verdict: Canva AI is the right pick for marketers and small businesses who want one tool. Firefly is for designers already in Creative Cloud who need legally safe AI in Photoshop and Illustrator.

dimensions.name: Design Templates
dimensions.explanation: Canva has thousands of templates for every format. Firefly is generation-focused with no template library.

dimensions.name: Pro Design Tools
dimensions.explanation: Firefly lives in Photoshop and Illustrator — pixel-level control, layers, and pro typography. Canva is consumer-grade.

dimensions.name: Image Quality
dimensions.explanation: Both produce solid images. Firefly trained on licensed Adobe Stock; Canva uses multiple models.

dimensions.name: Commercial Safety
dimensions.explanation: Firefly is explicitly trained on licensed content with indemnification on enterprise plans. Canva's outputs are usually safe but with less explicit guarantee.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Canva Pro is $15/month total. Adobe needs Creative Cloud at $23+/month plus Firefly credits.

dimensions.name: Learning Curve
dimensions.explanation: Canva is built for non-designers. Photoshop and Illustrator have a real learning curve.

quickPicks.label: Choose Canva AI if…
points: You're a marketer, small business, or non-designer
You want one tool for image, video, design, and copy
You don't already pay for Creative Cloud

quickPicks.label: Choose Adobe Firefly if…
points: You already use Photoshop, Illustrator, or Premiere
Commercial-safe output with indemnification matters
You need pro design control and pixel-level edits

title: Replit vs Cursor
subtitle: Browser IDE with agent vs AI-first desktop editor
verdict: Replit is for non-engineers prototyping full apps without setup. Cursor is for working engineers who want the best AI-in-the-editor experience.

dimensions.name: Setup Time
dimensions.explanation: Replit has zero setup — open browser, start building. Cursor needs a download and project setup, but it's still fast.

dimensions.name: Agent Capabilities
dimensions.explanation: Replit Agent builds and deploys whole apps from a prompt. Cursor's Composer is powerful but more granular.

dimensions.name: Code Editor Power
dimensions.explanation: Cursor is a full VS Code fork with all extensions. Replit's editor is fine but less powerful for complex projects.

dimensions.name: Deployment
dimensions.explanation: Replit deploys with one click. Cursor relies on you using Vercel, Netlify, or similar separately.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Both have $20/month tiers. Replit's agent burns credits faster on complex projects.

dimensions.name: Pro Workflows
dimensions.explanation: Cursor handles enterprise codebases, complex Git workflows, and pro debugging better.

quickPicks.label: Choose Replit if…
points: You're learning to code or shipping prototypes
You want zero setup and one-click deploy
You prefer a hosted browser environment

quickPicks.label: Choose Cursor if…
points: You're a working engineer with an existing codebase
You want pro-grade editor features alongside AI
You already have a deploy pipeline you like

title: Gamma vs Beautiful.ai
subtitle: AI-generated decks vs smart-template auto-layout
verdict: Gamma wins on speed of generation — type a prompt, get a deck. Beautiful.ai wins for execs and finance teams who want polished, predictable layouts.

dimensions.name: AI Generation
dimensions.explanation: Gamma generates a full deck from one prompt. Beautiful.ai has AI but expects more manual content entry.

dimensions.name: Template Polish
dimensions.explanation: Beautiful.ai's smart templates auto-balance text and charts. Gamma's themes feel more samey across decks.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Gamma has a real free tier with watermarked exports. Beautiful.ai has no free tier, only a 14-day trial.

dimensions.name: Charts & Data
dimensions.explanation: Beautiful.ai's chart library is stronger and more business-flavored. Gamma's charts are basic.

dimensions.name: Web Publishing
dimensions.explanation: Gamma decks publish as responsive web pages. Beautiful.ai exports to PPT but isn't web-native.

dimensions.name: Best Audience
dimensions.explanation: Gamma fits creators and small teams. Beautiful.ai fits sales, finance, and corporate audiences.

quickPicks.label: Choose Gamma if…
points: You want decks generated from a prompt in 30 seconds
Your decks live on the web or as shareable links
You need a real free tier

quickPicks.label: Choose Beautiful.ai if…
points: You're presenting to execs or finance teams
Charts and predictable layouts matter
You want corporate polish over creative flair

title: Quillbot vs Grammarly
subtitle: Best AI writing assistant in 2026?
verdict: Quillbot wins for paraphrasing and academic writing on a budget. Grammarly wins for grammar accuracy and works in more places. Many serious writers pay for both.

dimensions.name: Writing Quality
dimensions.explanation: Both produce solid, professional output for everyday writing. Quillbot's paraphrasing modes give more stylistic variety; Grammarly's tone detection is more nuanced.

dimensions.name: Ease of Use
dimensions.explanation: Grammarly's browser extension and desktop app are more polished and work in nearly every input box on the web. Quillbot's interface is functional but less seamless.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Quillbot Premium is $9.95/month vs Grammarly Premium at $30/month. With the annual discount, Quillbot drops to $4.17/month — meaningfully cheaper.

dimensions.name: Free Tier
dimensions.explanation: Quillbot's free tier supports 125-word paraphrasing plus full grammar checking. Grammarly's free tier covers basics but caps generative AI features tightly.

dimensions.name: Plagiarism Check
dimensions.explanation: Quillbot includes plagiarism checking on Premium with an academic-quality engine. Grammarly's plagiarism check is solid but only on Premium and feels slower.

dimensions.name: Grammar Checking
dimensions.explanation: Grammarly's grammar engine remains the gold standard — catches subtle issues like dangling modifiers and parallel structure that Quillbot misses.

dimensions.name: Paraphrasing
dimensions.explanation: Quillbot ships 7 paraphrasing modes (Standard, Fluency, Formal, Simple, Creative, Expand, Shorten). Grammarly has one rewrite mode that often produces generic output.

dimensions.name: Integrations
dimensions.explanation: Grammarly works in MS Word, Google Docs, Outlook, Slack, and dozens of native apps. Quillbot covers Word and Docs but lags everywhere else.

quickPicks.label: Choose Quillbot if…
points: You're a student or ESL writer needing affordable paraphrasing
You write academic content and need plagiarism checking included
You want 7 different rewrite modes to play with
Budget matters — Quillbot is roughly a third of Grammarly's price

quickPicks.label: Choose Grammarly if…
points: You write across many apps and want one consistent assistant
Grammar accuracy matters more than rewriting variety
You're a professional writer or work in business communication
Tone consistency across email, Slack, and Docs is important

title: Ideogram vs Midjourney
subtitle: Which AI image generator is better?
verdict: Midjourney wins on artistic quality and is the right pick for most creative work. Ideogram wins decisively when you need readable text inside images — posters, ads, quote graphics.

dimensions.name: Image Quality
dimensions.explanation: Midjourney V6/V7 produces visually stunning, painterly results that no other model matches. Ideogram is competent but rarely produces images you'd frame.

dimensions.name: Text in Images
dimensions.explanation: Ideogram is the gold standard for legible text in images — typography, posters, signage. Midjourney still struggles with anything beyond a few clean letters.

dimensions.name: Ease of Use
dimensions.explanation: Ideogram is a clean web app with simple controls. Midjourney's web interface launched late and Discord workflow remains clunky for newcomers.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Ideogram Plus is $8/month vs Midjourney's $10 entry. Midjourney's mid-tier ($30/month) gets pricier fast for serious commercial use.

dimensions.name: Free Tier
dimensions.explanation: Ideogram's free tier is generous — about 25 prompts per day at full quality. Midjourney has no free tier at all.

dimensions.name: Speed
dimensions.explanation: Midjourney's Fast mode generates in 30–60 seconds. Ideogram is comparable for text-heavy work but slower on detailed scenes.

dimensions.name: Artistic Styles
dimensions.explanation: Midjourney excels at stylized art — concept art, paintings, cinematic photos. Ideogram is improving but still feels limited in stylistic range.

dimensions.name: Commercial Rights
dimensions.explanation: Both grant commercial rights on paid plans. Midjourney requires Stealth mode ($60/month Pro) for full privacy on commercial work; Ideogram includes private generations starting on Plus.

quickPicks.label: Choose Ideogram if…
points: You design posters, ads, or anything with readable text
You want a generous free tier to experiment with
You prefer a clean web app over Discord
Pricing matters and your needs are mostly typography-focused

quickPicks.label: Choose Midjourney if…
points: You care about artistic and stylistic quality above all
You produce concept art, mood boards, or marketing visuals
You want a consistent visual look across an entire project (--sref)
You're willing to pay for the best output quality available

title: Murf AI vs ElevenLabs
subtitle: Best AI voice generator compared
verdict: ElevenLabs wins on voice naturalness and cloning quality. Murf wins for L&D, corporate training, and teams that need predictable multilingual output with editing tools.

dimensions.name: Voice Quality
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs voices are the most natural in the industry — fooled most listeners in blind tests. Murf voices are clean and professional but more obviously synthetic on emotional reads.

dimensions.name: Voice Variety
dimensions.explanation: Murf ships 200+ voices across 20+ languages. ElevenLabs has fewer stock voices but a community library of cloned voices that's effectively much larger.

dimensions.name: Ease of Use
dimensions.explanation: Murf's editor is built for creators — pronunciation editor, emphasis, pauses, all visual. ElevenLabs is faster for one-off TTS but has fewer fine-tuning controls in the UI.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs starts at $5/month for the Starter tier; Murf is $19/month. ElevenLabs scales more predictably for solo creators.

dimensions.name: Free Tier
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs offers 10,000 characters/month free; Murf only 10 minutes/month. ElevenLabs gives you more practical room to evaluate.

dimensions.name: Languages
dimensions.explanation: Murf supports 20+ languages, ElevenLabs supports 30+. Both produce native-quality output in major languages; ElevenLabs has slight edge in less-common ones.

dimensions.name: Custom Voice Cloning
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs clones voices from 1 minute of sample with industry-leading accuracy. Murf supports voice cloning but quality is one tier below.

dimensions.name: API Access
dimensions.explanation: ElevenLabs is API-first with strong SDKs and developer docs. Murf has an API but it's a secondary product, less polished for production use.

quickPicks.label: Choose Murf if…
points: You build L&D or corporate training videos at scale
Your team needs visual editing with emphasis and pauses
You need consistent multilingual voiceover for global content
Predictability and team workflow matter more than top-tier naturalness

quickPicks.label: Choose ElevenLabs if…
points: Voice quality is non-negotiable — you need the most natural output
You're cloning your own voice or characters for narration
You're building a voice product and need the best API
You want a generous free tier to experiment seriously

title: Synthesia vs HeyGen
subtitle: Best AI avatar video platform?
verdict: HeyGen wins on avatar lip-sync quality and translation. Synthesia wins for enterprise L&D with stronger compliance, templates, and scale features.

dimensions.name: Avatar Quality
dimensions.explanation: HeyGen's avatars have noticeably more natural lip sync and facial expressions. Synthesia avatars are professional but feel more synthetic on close-ups.

dimensions.name: Languages
dimensions.explanation: Synthesia supports 130+ languages with native-quality voiceovers. HeyGen covers 30+ but offers real-time translation that Synthesia doesn't.

dimensions.name: Ease of Use
dimensions.explanation: Synthesia's editor is built specifically for course creators — templates, screen recording, branching scenarios. HeyGen is simpler but more general-purpose.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Synthesia starts at $22/month; HeyGen Creator is $24/month. HeyGen's Pro tier ($72/mo) is steeper than Synthesia's mid-range plans for similar volume.

dimensions.name: Templates
dimensions.explanation: Synthesia ships hundreds of templates targeted at training, onboarding, and product education. HeyGen has fewer but more focused on marketing and sales.

dimensions.name: Custom Avatars
dimensions.explanation: HeyGen creates custom avatars from 2 minutes of footage with strong likeness. Synthesia's custom avatars require longer training footage and are pricier to commission.

dimensions.name: API Access
dimensions.explanation: Both have production-ready APIs. HeyGen's real-time translation API is unique; Synthesia has stronger enterprise compliance docs.

dimensions.name: Output Quality
dimensions.explanation: HeyGen's video output looks more lifelike for marketing and sales videos. Synthesia is more polished for training but less engaging for short-form content.

quickPicks.label: Choose Synthesia if…
points: You build training, onboarding, or compliance videos at enterprise scale
You need 130+ languages for global L&D programs
Templates and scenarios for instructional design matter
SOC 2 and GDPR compliance is required by your organization

quickPicks.label: Choose HeyGen if…
points: You produce marketing or sales videos and want lifelike avatars
Real-time video translation with mouth-sync is a key feature
You want a custom avatar of yourself or a spokesperson
Output engagement matters more than template breadth

title: OpusClip vs Descript
subtitle: Best AI video repurposing tool?
verdict: OpusClip wins for fast long-to-short repurposing and viral clip detection. Descript wins as a full editor — better for creators who do everything from recording to publishing in one tool.

dimensions.name: Clip Quality
dimensions.explanation: OpusClip's ClipAnything 2.0 picks viral moments based on engagement scoring. Descript can clip but you have to mark moments manually.

dimensions.name: Ease of Use
dimensions.explanation: OpusClip is single-purpose — paste a YouTube URL, get clips. Descript is more powerful but has a real learning curve for transcript editing.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: OpusClip Pro is $19/month with unlimited exports and HD. Descript Creator is $12/month but jumps to $24 for the features podcasters actually need.

dimensions.name: Speed
dimensions.explanation: OpusClip processes a 1-hour video into 10+ short clips in under 5 minutes. Descript is fast but you do the editing — total time longer end-to-end.

dimensions.name: Caption Quality
dimensions.explanation: Both produce solid captions. OpusClip styles auto-generate to match short-form trends; Descript gives more manual control over typography.

dimensions.name: Editing Features
dimensions.explanation: Descript is a full multi-track editor with screen recording, voice cloning, and Studio Sound. OpusClip is repurpose-focused with limited deep editing.

dimensions.name: Platform Support
dimensions.explanation: OpusClip exports for TikTok, Reels, Shorts, and Twitter. Descript exports anywhere — it's an editor first. Different paths to the same destination.

quickPicks.label: Choose OpusClip if…
points: You're repurposing podcasts or long YouTube videos into shorts
You want AI to pick the most viral moments automatically
Speed matters — you need clips in minutes, not hours
You don't need to record or edit from scratch

quickPicks.label: Choose Descript if…
points: You record, edit, and publish full long-form video or podcasts
Transcript-based editing is core to your workflow
You need a single tool for the full creator pipeline
Studio Sound and voice cloning are useful for your work

title: Notion vs Mem AI
subtitle: Best AI note-taking app?
verdict: Notion wins for teams, structured projects, and rich documents. Mem AI wins for solo knowledge workers who hate manual organization and want AI to handle filing.

dimensions.name: AI Features
dimensions.explanation: Mem AI is AI-native — semantic search, auto-tagging, and chat across your notes work without setup. Notion AI is bolted on and requires you to invoke it.

dimensions.name: Organization
dimensions.explanation: Notion's databases, templates, and nested pages give you real structure. Mem favors a flat, AI-organized model that some users find harder to navigate.

dimensions.name: Ease of Use
dimensions.explanation: Notion has a steeper learning curve but pays off for power users. Mem is faster to onboard but less flexible once you outgrow basic notes.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Notion is $10/user/month base + $10/user/month for AI ($20 total). Mem AI is $14.99/month all-in. For solo users Mem is slightly cheaper; for teams Notion wins.

dimensions.name: Collaboration
dimensions.explanation: Notion's real-time collaboration, comments, and team workspaces are best-in-class. Mem is improving on team features but is fundamentally a personal tool.

dimensions.name: Search Quality
dimensions.explanation: Mem's semantic search finds notes by meaning, not just keywords. Notion's search is keyword-based with AI as an option, slower in practice.

dimensions.name: Mobile Experience
dimensions.explanation: Neither app has best-in-class mobile. Notion's mobile lags on big workspaces; Mem's mobile capture is fast but editing is constrained.

quickPicks.label: Choose Notion if…
points: You work in a team and need shared docs, wikis, or projects
You want databases, kanban boards, and structured pages
Templates and integrations matter for your workflow
You'll use it for project management, not just notes

quickPicks.label: Choose Mem AI if…
points: You're a solo knowledge worker drowning in unorganized notes
You want AI to handle tagging and connections automatically
Semantic search ('find that thing about X') is more important than structure
You hate spending time organizing — you just want to capture and recall

title: Tabnine vs GitHub Copilot
subtitle: Best AI code completion tool?
verdict: GitHub Copilot wins on suggestion quality, integrations, and value for individual developers. Tabnine wins for enterprise teams with strict privacy requirements that need self-hosting.

dimensions.name: Code Quality
dimensions.explanation: Copilot's underlying models (GPT-4o, Claude) produce noticeably better suggestions for everyday coding tasks. Tabnine's quality is fine but trails on complex problems.

dimensions.name: IDE Support
dimensions.explanation: Both support VS Code, JetBrains, Vim, Eclipse, and Visual Studio. Copilot has slightly better IntelliJ integration; Tabnine has stronger Eclipse support.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Copilot is $10/month for individuals, free for verified students. Tabnine Pro is $12/user/month with no individual student pricing.

dimensions.name: Free Tier
dimensions.explanation: Copilot has no general free tier but is free for students and OSS maintainers. Tabnine's free tier limits you to basic completions after 90 days.

dimensions.name: Privacy
dimensions.explanation: Tabnine is the clear winner for privacy — self-hostable, custom models trained on your code, no data sharing. Copilot routes through Microsoft's infrastructure.

dimensions.name: Language Support
dimensions.explanation: Both support 30+ programming languages with reasonable quality. Copilot edges ahead on TypeScript and Python; Tabnine on legacy languages like COBOL and Fortran.

dimensions.name: Speed
dimensions.explanation: Suggestion latency is similar — both target sub-300ms. Tabnine's local model option is faster offline; Copilot is faster on cold starts.

dimensions.name: Accuracy
dimensions.explanation: Copilot's suggestions match developer intent more often, especially on framework-specific code. Tabnine improves with custom training but base accuracy is lower.

quickPicks.label: Choose Tabnine if…
points: Your company has strict data privacy or compliance requirements
You need to train AI on your private code without it leaving your network
You work in regulated industries — finance, healthcare, defense
Self-hosting is mandatory for security or sovereignty reasons

quickPicks.label: Choose GitHub Copilot if…
points: You're an individual developer or small team without privacy constraints
You want the highest-quality AI suggestions available
You're a verified student or OSS maintainer (free)
You live in GitHub PRs and want AI tied tightly to your repo

title: Writesonic vs Jasper
subtitle: Best AI marketing copy tool?
verdict: Jasper wins for enterprise teams and brand voice consistency. Writesonic wins for SEO content marketers who need long-form drafts plus on-page optimization in one tool.

dimensions.name: Content Quality
dimensions.explanation: Both produce solid marketing copy that needs editing. Jasper feels more polished for short-form ads; Writesonic edges ahead on long-form articles thanks to its built-in research.

dimensions.name: Templates
dimensions.explanation: Jasper ships 50+ marketing templates with strong customization. Writesonic's templates are decent but less brand-flexible.

dimensions.name: Ease of Use
dimensions.explanation: Writesonic's UI is cleaner and faster to onboard. Jasper has more features but the workflow can feel overwhelming for new users.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Writesonic starts at $19/month with a free tier. Jasper has no free tier and starts at $49/month — more than 2x the entry price.

dimensions.name: SEO Features
dimensions.explanation: Writesonic includes SEO scoring, keyword optimization, and competitor analysis natively. Jasper requires Surfer SEO integration ($69/mo extra) for the same.

dimensions.name: Language Support
dimensions.explanation: Writesonic supports 25+ languages with native quality. Jasper supports 30+ but quality drops noticeably outside English.

dimensions.name: Team Features
dimensions.explanation: Jasper's team workspaces, brand voice training, and SSO/approval workflows are enterprise-ready. Writesonic's team features are basic by comparison.

quickPicks.label: Choose Writesonic if…
points: SEO content is core to your strategy — you need scoring built in
You publish in multiple languages
Long-form articles are most of your output
You want a real free tier and a lower entry price

quickPicks.label: Choose Jasper if…
points: You're an agency or large marketing team
Brand voice consistency across many writers is critical
You need approval workflows, SSO, and enterprise admin
Templates and short-form ad copy dominate your work

title: Udio vs Suno
subtitle: Best AI music generator?
verdict: Suno wins for speed, ease, and viral moments — type a prompt, get a song. Udio wins for songwriters who care about vocal nuance and longer-form arrangement.

dimensions.name: Music Quality
dimensions.explanation: Udio produces more polished, dynamic arrangements with realistic vocals. Suno is great but vocals occasionally sound auto-tuned on long held notes.

dimensions.name: Genre Variety
dimensions.explanation: Both cover the major genres well. Udio is slightly stronger on jazz, soul, and orchestral; Suno wins on pop, electronic, and country.

dimensions.name: Ease of Use
dimensions.explanation: Suno's UI is faster — type a prompt, get a complete song. Udio offers more controls and section-based editing, which is powerful but slower for casual users.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Both start free with daily credits and scale to ~$10–30/month. Udio gives more credits for the same money on Pro plans.

dimensions.name: Free Tier
dimensions.explanation: Udio offers 600 credits/month free. Suno's free tier is 50 credits/day (~1,500/month) but with stricter commercial limits.

dimensions.name: Lyric Generation
dimensions.explanation: Both can write lyrics from a prompt or accept your own. Suno's are catchier; Udio's are more literary. Pick based on style preference.

dimensions.name: Commercial Rights
dimensions.explanation: Both gate commercial rights behind paid tiers. Both face active legal questions about training data — neither offers full indemnification yet.

quickPicks.label: Choose Udio if…
points: You're a songwriter who cares about vocal nuance and arrangement
You want a generous free tier (600 credits/month)
Section-based editing matters — you want bridges, breakdowns, outros
Quality of the final track is more important than speed

quickPicks.label: Choose Suno if…
points: You want a song from a one-line prompt in 30 seconds
You're scoring video, podcasts, or content that needs music quickly
Pop, electronic, and country are your main genres
Stem separation is important for further mixing in a DAW

title: Reclaim AI vs Motion
subtitle: Best AI calendar and scheduling app?
verdict: Reclaim wins for individual contributors and free tier users who mostly defend focus time. Motion wins for solo founders and freelancers juggling many tasks across multiple calendars.

dimensions.name: Scheduling Intelligence
dimensions.explanation: Motion's AI rebuilds your day automatically when meetings change — true 'living calendar.' Reclaim auto-schedules tasks but is less aggressive about rebalancing.

dimensions.name: Calendar Integration
dimensions.explanation: Motion supports Google Calendar AND Outlook with full feature parity. Reclaim's Outlook support exists but feels secondary to its Google Calendar focus.

dimensions.name: Task Management
dimensions.explanation: Motion combines tasks, projects, and calendar in one app — replaces Asana for small teams. Reclaim's task features are lighter and best paired with another PM tool.

dimensions.name: Ease of Use
dimensions.explanation: Reclaim's setup is simpler — habits, tasks, 1:1s. Motion has a steeper learning curve to configure properly but rewards the investment.

dimensions.name: Pricing
dimensions.explanation: Reclaim has a free tier and starts at $10/month for Pro. Motion has no free plan — minimum $19/month and pricier for teams.

dimensions.name: Team Features
dimensions.explanation: Motion handles team scheduling, project management, and capacity planning. Reclaim's team features are limited to shared 1:1 scheduling and meeting links.

dimensions.name: Mobile Experience
dimensions.explanation: Neither app has best-in-class mobile. Both work but feel like companions to the desktop experience, not full standalone apps.

quickPicks.label: Choose Reclaim if…
points: You mostly want to defend focus time and auto-schedule habits
You're a Google Calendar power user
You want a real free tier to evaluate before committing
Your task management lives elsewhere (Todoist, Linear, etc.)

quickPicks.label: Choose Motion if…
points: You're a solo founder or freelancer juggling many tasks daily
You want one tool for tasks, projects, and calendar
You use Outlook OR you switch between Outlook and Google
You'll pay for AI that aggressively rebuilds your day around changes
